text:
I was on a plane yesterday and it made me realise that phenomenologically speaking, the feeling of surrendering to emotions and feeling the energetic current underneath them feel similar to the rush you feel when the plane is taking off.

I used to find the former quite intense. it would rise up my body once and then immediately get clamped down in my hips, then over months of working with it, it moved to my solar plexus, and with even more time, the clamping spot kept moving further up my body, and the energy could be felt for a bit longer.

sometimes it releases but feels incomplete, other times it releases fully and there’s a distinct shift in my experience. I feel some mix of relief, joy, gratitude and an impulse to go out there and live/do/act.

text:
I’ve been exploring a known trigger that comes up in different contexts and going down the layers, I would sometimes end up in this odd place where I would say “there’s nothing there”.

like it would feel like an absence of stimuli, but not exactly nothing either, it also didn’t feel like any blanking or dissociation I’ve experienced, I was fully present. It would just feel mundane.

nothing would happen past that point and I would just get on with my day.

recently though, I stayed in that place. I thought ok, there’s nothing happening, it’s mundane, so be it, I’m just gonna stay here and not do anything.

I noticed myself wanting to reach out, to seek out stimuli or to get help. I wanted something, anything. there was a quiet helplessness to it.

then I closed my eyes and I felt my body sinking into this dark place, like the underworld. it was icky, uncomfortable and subtly graphic.

I thought oh I know this place. it’s a common experience I’ve had on “bad” psychedelic trips. something I could never make sense of and found torturous. this time though experiencing it more subtly while sober and feeling a lot of let’s say self energy, it was quite easy to be with.

at some point, my womb area and hip flexors lighted up like they were raised (not flush with the surface of my body)

and I got the urge to get up (I’d been dancing, crying, moving a bunch on the lead up to it so it felt like picking up where I left off)

I started moving my body and stretching intuitively and suddenly I burst out crying out of nowhere. the transition was super abrupt and lacked any story whatsoever. I kept weeping and crying uncontrollably until it came to a stop naturally.

there are two things that interest me about this experience. one is that mundane place. it was neither an emotion, nor a dissociation that could be recognised as one. turns out it was extremely sneaky.

secondly, it’s fascinating that the layer that showed up after all the preceding layers, underneath the sneaky mundane place, is the first place that I’ve been thrown into on psychedelics. ofc this is a known phenomenon but it’s interesting to experience it so clearly first hand.

text:
great question and it’s probably above my pay grade…

but ‘data’ is the closet thing that comes to mind, usually in order to mobilise you.

like imagine you take a bite of your grandmother’s food. 

there’s an instant qualia around its taste, texture, temperature, wetness…

but there’s all this data constructed on top of that raw sense, like enjoyment, memories of childhood, love towards grandma, gratitude, an urge to take another bite (mobilise) and so on.

they all get generated on top of the raw sensory experience.

what do you think constructedness is?

text:
had a dating situation recently that brought up a lot of emotions and I was telling a new friend about it, about how much I was able to learn and process some of my attachment hangups because of it and how grateful I was.

she said oh that’s nice that you can look at the bright side, but like no, that’s far from the truth. I’m not “looking” at the bright side. I am actively benefiting from it.

and I’m genuinely excited and nerding out about this stuff even though it entails crying on my bedroom floor and facing lots of difficult emotions.

the material was already there and it got to be surfaced. how helpful.

I get to see what I’m projecting on to the other person, what dormant “hope” is getting awakened in me that’s shaping my response to them, and how I can decouple that from the person in front of me. so I can be more free in future interactions. how cool.

ok 7 months later, here are some working “answers” I’ve come up with to the question of why be in a committed romantic relationship anyway:

- doing things in pairs is really fun
- mutual sensemaking with someone you deeply know and respect can be v helpful in navigating life
- deep commitment and devotion to someone can be v fulfilling
- learning how to navigate conflict, developing capacity for doing rupture well and building a deeply healthy relationship… nerding out about all of that feels deeply enriching and like a hobby for me 

I still don’t feel like these necessarily answer my original questions but they are a helpful starting point…

text:
I was a picky eater as a child. only liked basic foods like meat, some dairy and simple carbs. 

my dad being an extremely patient person was very persistent at getting me to try things over and over again until I would like them.

I don't remember most of the specific moments well because I was young but I remember olives, the final boss. it took me until I was almost 20 before I liked them.

he would put the olive in his mouth and say here try it it's so yummy, to which I would say but I've tried it so many times and it's disgusting and he'd say but try it again, this one's not bitter at all, and somehow his earnestness would break me and I'd begrudgingly put one in my mouth. until... voila.

text:
if you feel a cocktail of emotions that manifests as overwhelm, restlessness, feeling lost etc, here’s one way to explore it:

grab a piece of paper, write each thing down and use arrows to distinguish between them (eg: I feel lost -> I’m scared I won’t know what to do -> I’m ashamed) 

then pick the first item and/or the item that has the most arrows pointing back to it, and then sit with that feeling and don’t grab the thoughts that come up.

it will often result in some kind of shift. maybe relief, emotional expression, clarity, joy etc.

some thoughts on procrastination after learning to procrastinate less:

first I wanna talk about the most common (and often more sticky) causes of procrastination and how to unblock oneself. afterwards I will share other noteworthy causes:

raw.full_text:
some thoughts on procrastination after learning to procrastinate less:

first I wanna talk about the most common (and often more sticky) causes of procrastination and how to unblock oneself. afterwards I will share other noteworthy causes:

text:
the common causes:

procrastination is often masking fear, usually one of these:

- fear of uncertainty
- fear of shame
- fear of helplessness

- and sometimes it's just aversion. "I don't wannaaa"  (eg: filing taxes, cleaning etc)

text:
let's break these down:

fear of uncertainty: this happens because the task is ambiguous or has some unknowns in it. and some nervous systems find uncertainty particularly uncomfortable (common in neurodivergence)

text: fear of helplessness: the task or decision is being put off because taking action or deciding will lock you out of other potential outcomes so there's an anticipation of regret and/or not being able to undo the decision/action.
